steps.title: Fastest path: use the circle facts first
steps.content: For coordinate-circle questions, read the center and radius from the given information first. Use equal radii, diameters, tangents, and arcs before doing algebra.

steps.title: Find L's coordinates and compute angle JKL
steps.content:
Since L = (x, -1) lies on the unit circle, we apply the circle equation:
$$x^2 + (-1)^2 = 1$$
$$x^2 = 0 \implies x = 0$$
So L = (0, -1). This makes J = (0, 1) and L = (0, -1) diametrically opposite points on the circle. The angle at center K between them is:
$$\angle JKL = \pi \text{ radians}$$

steps.title: Find all possible positive angle measures
steps.content:
Since angles can be measured by adding full rotations (multiples of $2\pi$), all positive measures of angle JKL have the form:
$$\pi + 2\pi k = (2k+1)\pi \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$
This gives the sequence: $\pi$, $3\pi$, $5\pi$, $7\pi$, $9\pi$, $11\pi$, $13\pi$,... all odd multiples of $\pi$.

steps.title: Identify which choice is an odd multiple of pi
steps.content:
• A) $\frac{17\pi}{2} = 8.5\pi$ is not an integer multiple of $\pi$.
  • B) $\frac{19\pi}{2} = 9.5\pi$ is not an integer multiple of $\pi$.
  • C) $12\pi$ is an even multiple of $\pi$ because $12 = 2\times 6$.
  • D) $13\pi$ is an odd multiple of $\pi$, since $13 = 2(6) + 1$.
That confirms D is correct.
